title: Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations 2002
chapters: SI 2002/2677. 21 Regulations + 6 Schedules. Made under HSWA 1974 ss.15(1), 15(2), 15(3)(a), 15(3)(c), 15(4), 15(5)(a), 80(1), 82(3)(a). Implements multiple EU Directives on chemical agents, carcinogens, and biological agents. Supported by HSE ACOP L5 (6th edition). The Regulations impose a comprehensive duty on employers to ASSESS, PREVENT or CONTROL, MONITOR, SURVEY, and TRAIN in relation to any substance hazardous to health encountered in the workplace. Breach is a criminal offence — unlimited fine on indictment.

parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Made by the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions under the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Replaced COSHH 1999 (SI 1999/437), which itself replaced the original COSHH 1988. The 2002 Regulations consolidated and strengthened the framework. Amended in 2004 (SI 2004/3386) to incorporate updated EU obligations on carcinogens and biological agents. COSHH applies to virtually every workplace where any hazardous substance is used, produced, stored, or encountered — from industrial chemicals to everyday cleaning products, flour dust to wood dust, biological agents in healthcare to fumes in welding shops.

significantAmendments.instrument: Control of Substances Hazardous to Health (Amendment) Regulations 2004 (SI 2004/3386)
significantAmendments.effect: Updated provisions on carcinogens, mutagens, and biological agents to implement revised EU Directives. Strengthened the prohibition on certain substances and the requirements for health surveillance.

significantAmendments.instrument: HSE EH40/2005 Workplace Exposure Limits (4th edition, as amended 2020)
significantAmendments.effect: Current table of Workplace Exposure Limits (WELs) — legally enforceable maximum concentrations of hazardous substances in workplace air, expressed as 8-hour time-weighted averages (TWAs) and 15-minute short-term exposure limits (STELs). Updated in 2020 to incorporate binding EU occupational exposure limit values.

precedingDocuments.title: Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 (parent Act)
precedingDocuments.significance: HSWA s.2 imposes the overarching duty. COSHH provides the specific, detailed framework for controlling exposure to hazardous substances — one of the most significant categories of workplace health risk.

precedingDocuments.title: Substances NOT covered by COSHH (separate regulations)
precedingDocuments.significance: COSHH does NOT cover: lead at work (Control of Lead at Work Regulations 2002), asbestos (Control of Asbestos Regulations 2012), or radioactive substances (Ionising Radiations Regulations 2017). These have their own dedicated, more stringent regulatory frameworks. The HR manager must know which framework applies to which substance.

leadingCases.name: Limited higher court case law — enforcement primarily through HSE prosecution
leadingCases.principle: COSHH enforcement is primarily through HSE inspection, improvement notices, prohibition notices, and prosecution. Significant COSHH prosecutions typically involve occupational disease (mesothelioma, occupational asthma, dermatitis) where the employer failed to assess, prevent, or control exposure over a sustained period. Sentencing follows the Sentencing Council Health and Safety Offences Guidelines (2016) — fines for large organisations can reach millions of pounds for serious harm categories.
leadingCases.significance: The real enforcement risk in COSHH is the combination of criminal prosecution AND civil claims for occupational disease. An employee who develops occupational asthma, dermatitis, or cancer from workplace exposure can pursue both: a criminal prosecution by the HSE and a civil claim for personal injury. The damages in occupational disease claims can be substantial — especially for cancer and chronic lung disease.

parts.title: Part 1 — Scope: What Is a 'Substance Hazardous to Health' and Who Must Comply

sections.title: Definition of hazardous substances, scope of the Regulations, and the employer's overarching duty
sections.summary: COSHH applies to a vast range of substances encountered in the workplace — far broader than most employers realise. A 'substance hazardous to health' includes: (a) any substance classified as very toxic, toxic, harmful, corrosive, or irritant under the CLP Regulation, (b) any substance with a Workplace Exposure Limit (WEL) listed in EH40, (c) any biological agent (bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites) connected to work, (d) any dust present in substantial concentrations (including 'nuisance dust' above 10 mg/m³ inhalable or 4 mg/m³ respirable), (e) any other substance which, because of its chemical or toxicological properties and the way it is used, creates a risk to health. This means COSHH covers: industrial chemicals, cleaning products (bleach, disinfectants, degreasers), paints and solvents, adhesives, welding fumes, flour dust, wood dust, silica dust, cement, biological agents in healthcare, and even substances generated by work processes (e.g., fumes from soldering). Every employer — from a factory to an office — will have SOME hazardous substances within scope.
sections.text:
WHAT IS COVERED:
(1) Chemicals classified under CLP Regulation with hazard pictograms (skull and crossbones, exclamation mark, corrosion, health hazard, etc.).
(2) Substances with a WEL in EH40/2005 — over 500 substances with legally enforceable exposure limits.
(3) Biological agents — bacteria, viruses, parasites, fungi. Classified into 4 hazard groups (Group 1 = unlikely to cause disease → Group 4 = serious disease, no effective treatment, e.g., Ebola).
(4) Dust of ANY kind in substantial concentrations — including wood dust, flour dust, grain dust, silica dust, cement dust.
(5) Any substance that creates a health risk through its use — even if not classified, if it can cause harm.

WHAT IS NOT COVERED (separate regulations):
— Lead (Control of Lead at Work Regulations 2002)
— Asbestos (Control of Asbestos Regulations 2012)
— Radioactive substances (Ionising Radiations Regulations 2017)
— Substances hazardous only because of their physical properties (flammable, explosive — covered by DSEAR 2002)

WHO MUST COMPLY:
Every employer who uses, stores, produces, or whose work processes generate any substance hazardous to health. This includes:
— Factories, workshops, laboratories
— Construction sites (cement dust, silica, solvents)
— Healthcare settings (biological agents, cleaning chemicals, medicines)
— Offices (cleaning products, printer toner, correction fluid)
— Hospitality (cleaning chemicals, cooking fumes)
— Agriculture (pesticides, grain dust, biological agents)
— Hairdressing (hair dyes, bleaching agents — occupational dermatitis is a significant risk)

The duty extends to protecting EMPLOYEES, other workers, and NON-EMPLOYEES (visitors, members of the public) who may be exposed.

SAFETY DATA SHEETS (SDS) — WHAT THEY ARE AND HOW TO READ THEM:

A Safety Data Sheet is a standardised 16-section document that the SUPPLIER of a hazardous substance must provide to the employer. It contains all the information needed to conduct a COSHH assessment for that substance. The employer MUST obtain an SDS for every classified substance used in the workplace.

The 16 sections of an SDS (CLP/REACH format):
1. Identification of the substance and supplier
2. HAZARDS IDENTIFICATION — the hazard statements (H-codes) that summarise the key risks. THIS IS THE MOST IMPORTANT SECTION for initial COSHH assessment.
3. Composition/information on ingredients
4. First-aid measures
5. Fire-fighting measures
6. Accidental release measures (spill response)
7. HANDLING AND STORAGE — how to use and store the substance safely
8. EXPOSURE CONTROLS / PERSONAL PROTECTION — Workplace Exposure Limits (WELs) and recommended PPE. THIS IS CRITICAL for determining control measures.
9. Physical and chemical properties
10. Stability and reactivity
11. TOXICOLOGICAL INFORMATION — how the substance can harm health, routes of exposure, symptoms. ESSENTIAL for understanding the risk.
12. Ecological information
13. Disposal considerations
14. Transport information
15. Regulatory information
16. Other information

For a COSHH assessment, focus on sections 2, 7, 8, and 11. These tell you: what the hazards are (s.2), how to handle safely (s.7), what WELs and PPE apply (s.8), and how the substance can harm health (s.11).

'WE ONLY USE BLEACH AND WASHING-UP LIQUID — DO WE NEED COSHH?'

YES. Everyday products are hazardous substances:
— Bleach (sodium hypochlorite): corrosive, irritant to eyes and skin. GHS pictograms: corrosion, exclamation mark. COSHH assessment required.
— Dishwasher tablets/powder: irritant, may cause serious eye damage. COSHH assessment required.
— Anti-bacterial spray: may contain quaternary ammonium compounds — respiratory sensitiser in some formulations. COSHH assessment required.
— Oven cleaner: highly corrosive (sodium hydroxide). COSHH assessment required.
— Printer toner: respirable dust, may irritate airways. COSHH assessment required.
— Hand sanitiser (alcohol-based): flammable, skin irritant with prolonged use. COSHH assessment required.

The COSHH assessment for a low-risk office cleaning product will be SIMPLE — it may take 15 minutes per product using the HSE COSHH Essentials tool. But it must EXIST. An employer who has never assessed their cleaning products is in breach of Reg.6.
sections.keyPrinciple: COSHH is not just for heavy industry. Every workplace has hazardous substances — even an office uses cleaning products. The question is not WHETHER COSHH applies but WHAT substances in this specific workplace are within scope and HOW the employer is controlling exposure. The HR manager should ensure that every workplace has a COSHH assessment, not assume that 'we don't use chemicals'.
refs: CLP Regulation (Classification, Labelling and Packaging — hazard classification)
EH40/2005 (Workplace Exposure Limits — 4th edition, as amended 2020)
HSE ACOP L5 (6th edition — COSHH approved code of practice)
Control of Lead at Work Regulations 2002 (separate framework)
Control of Asbestos Regulations 2012 (separate framework)
DSEAR 2002 (Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations — physical hazards)
commonErrors: Assuming COSHH only applies to factories or laboratories — EVERY workplace has hazardous substances. Offices use cleaning products. Restaurants use degreasers. Hairdressers use dyes. Construction uses cement. The question is what substances and what controls, not whether COSHH applies

Not knowing the difference between COSHH, lead, and asbestos regulations — lead and asbestos have their own, MORE STRINGENT regulations. Using a COSHH assessment for asbestos is inadequate and potentially dangerous

Not identifying substances generated by work processes — COSHH covers not only substances brought into the workplace but also substances CREATED by work (welding fumes, solder fumes, wood dust from cutting, flour dust from baking). These process-generated substances are often overlooked
Failing to protect non-employees — COSHH requires protection of anyone who may be exposed, including visitors, contractors, customers, and members of the public

Not obtaining Safety Data Sheets from suppliers — the supplier is LEGALLY OBLIGED to provide an SDS for any classified substance. If the supplier has not provided one, the employer must REQUEST it. An SDS is the foundation of a COSHH assessment and cannot be skipped

parts.title: Part 2 — The Eight Steps of COSHH Management

sections.title: The structured COSHH management framework — assess, prevent, control, monitor, survey, plan, train, record
sections.summary: COSHH compliance is structured around EIGHT STEPS that form a management cycle. Every employer must work through all eight steps for every hazardous substance in the workplace. This is not a one-time exercise — it is a continuous cycle that must be reviewed regularly, and immediately whenever there is a change in substances used, processes, personnel, or when monitoring or health surveillance reveals a problem.
sections.text:
STEP 1 — ASSESS THE RISKS (Reg.6):
(a) Identify every substance hazardous to health used in, produced by, or arising from work activities.
(b) Obtain Safety Data Sheets (SDS) from suppliers for all classified substances.
(c) For each substance, assess: who is exposed, how they are exposed (inhalation, skin contact, ingestion), how much exposure occurs, how often, and for how long.
(d) Compare exposure levels against WELs (EH40) where applicable.
(e) Identify vulnerable workers: young workers, pregnant women, those with pre-existing conditions (asthma, dermatitis, immunosuppression).
(f) Record the assessment in writing (mandatory if 5+ employees).
(g) Review whenever there is reason to believe the assessment is no longer valid, or at regular intervals.

STEP 2 — PREVENT OR CONTROL EXPOSURE (Reg.7):
Apply the HIERARCHY OF CONTROL in strict order:
(a) ELIMINATE the substance — can the process be changed to remove the need for the hazardous substance entirely?
(b) SUBSTITUTE with a less hazardous substance — can a safer alternative be used?
(c) ISOLATE the process — enclose the process so workers are not exposed (e.g., glove boxes, enclosed mixing systems).
(d) ENGINEERING CONTROLS — local exhaust ventilation (LEV), fume cupboards, extraction systems to capture the substance at source.
(e) ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS — reduce the number of workers exposed, reduce the duration of exposure, job rotation, safe systems of work.
(f) PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT (PPE) — respirators, gloves, goggles, protective clothing. PPE is the LAST RESORT, not the first. An employer who relies on PPE without first considering elimination, substitution, isolation, and engineering controls is not complying with COSHH.

For CARCINOGENS, MUTAGENS, and ASTHMAGENS: the duty is to PREVENT exposure entirely if reasonably practicable. If prevention is not reasonably practicable, exposure must be reduced to As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP) — a more stringent standard than 'adequate control'.

STEP 3 — ENSURE CONTROLS ARE USED AND MAINTAINED (Reg.8-9):
(a) Employer must ensure control measures are properly used.
(b) Employees must use control measures correctly and report defects.
(c) LEV systems must be examined and tested at least every 14 months (or as specified).
(d) PPE must be maintained, stored properly, and replaced when defective.
(e) Maintenance records must be kept for at least 5 years.

STEP 4 — MONITOR EXPOSURE (Reg.10):
(a) Where the risk assessment indicates monitoring is necessary, the employer must carry out workplace exposure monitoring.
(b) Monitoring may involve air sampling, surface contamination checks, or biological monitoring (testing employees' blood or urine for substance levels).
(c) Monitoring records must be kept for at least 5 years (40 YEARS for personal exposure records of identifiable employees).
(d) Mandatory monitoring is required for certain substances (vinyl chloride, specific lead processes).

STEP 5 — HEALTH SURVEILLANCE (Reg.11):
(a) Required where: a known disease is linked to the substance, the disease can be detected and treated early, and workplace conditions make the disease likely.
(b) Examples: lung function tests for workers exposed to flour dust or isocyanates, skin checks for workers handling sensitisers, audiometry for noise-exposed workers (separate regulations but COSHH principle applies).
(c) Health surveillance records must be kept for at least 40 YEARS.
(d) Results must be made available to the employee and (in anonymised form) to employee representatives.
(e) If health surveillance reveals a disease or adverse health effect, the employer must review the risk assessment and controls.

STEP 6 — PREPARE PLANS FOR ACCIDENTS AND EMERGENCIES (Reg.13):
(a) Where work involves a risk of serious accident, incident, or emergency from a hazardous substance, the employer must have plans and procedures in place.
(b) These must include: procedures for dealing with spills, leaks, or releases; first aid arrangements for substance-specific injuries; emergency evacuation procedures.
(c) Plans must be tested and rehearsed at appropriate intervals.

STEP 7 — PROVIDE INFORMATION, INSTRUCTION, AND TRAINING (Reg.12):
(a) Employees exposed to hazardous substances must be given information about: the substances they work with, the risks to their health, the control measures in place, the correct use of PPE, the results of monitoring and health surveillance (in anonymised form), and emergency procedures.
(b) Training must be provided before exposure commences and updated when changes occur.
(c) Training must be in a language and format the employee can understand.

STEP 8 — KEEP RECORDS:
(a) COSHH assessments: retain for review and inspection.
(b) LEV testing records: at least 5 years.
(c) Monitoring records: at least 5 years (40 years for personal exposure records).
(d) Health surveillance records: at least 40 years.
(e) Training records: retain as evidence of compliance.
sections.keyPrinciple: COSHH is a MANAGEMENT SYSTEM, not a paperwork exercise. The eight steps form a cycle: assess → prevent/control → maintain → monitor → survey → plan → train → record → reassess. An employer who completes the assessment but fails to implement controls, or implements controls but fails to monitor, or monitors but fails to act on the results, is not compliant. The HR manager must ensure the SYSTEM is functioning, not just that the FORMS exist.
refs: HSE ACOP L5 (6th edition) — detailed guidance on each step
EH40/2005 (WELs — mandatory exposure limits)
HSE COSHH Essentials (online tool for SME COSHH assessments)
HSE LEV guidance (local exhaust ventilation testing requirements)
Sentencing Council Health and Safety Offences Guidelines (fine levels for COSHH breaches)
commonErrors: Treating the COSHH assessment as a one-off exercise — assessments must be reviewed regularly and whenever there is a change in substances, processes, or findings from monitoring/surveillance

Jumping to PPE without considering the hierarchy of control — PPE is the LAST RESORT. An employer who gives workers gloves and masks without first considering whether the hazardous substance can be eliminated, substituted, or controlled by engineering means is NOT compliant

Not keeping health surveillance records for 40 years — this is one of the longest record retention requirements in UK employment law. Health surveillance records for identifiable employees must be kept for 40 years because occupational diseases (particularly cancer and chronic lung disease) may not manifest for decades after exposure

Not monitoring exposure where the risk assessment indicates it is necessary — monitoring is not optional where the assessment shows it is needed. Relying solely on the initial assessment without monitoring actual exposure levels is a common failure

Not providing substance-specific training — generic 'health and safety awareness' training does not satisfy COSHH Reg.12. Training must be specific to the substances the employee works with, the routes of exposure, the controls in place, and the correct use of PPE
Failing to have emergency plans for spills and releases — an employer who uses quantities of hazardous substances without a spill response procedure is in breach of Reg.13

Not reviewing the assessment when health surveillance reveals a problem — if an employee develops dermatitis, occupational asthma, or another health condition linked to a workplace substance, this is EVIDENCE that the controls are inadequate. The assessment must be reviewed immediately

Not using the HSE COSHH Essentials online tool for simple assessments — the HSE provides a free, step-by-step online tool (coshh-tool.hse.gov.uk) that generates control guidance based on the substance's hazard classification and how it is used. For SMEs with low-risk substances, this is the most efficient route to a compliant assessment

parts.title: Part 3 — The Employee's Perspective: Rights, Obligations, and What to Do When You're Harmed

sections.num: Regs.8(2), 12 + cross-reference: ERA 1996 + civil claims + IIDB
sections.title: Employee obligations, employee rights, and the journey after diagnosis of an occupational disease
sections.summary: COSHH is not just an employer obligation — employees have both DUTIES and RIGHTS. The employee must use control measures correctly, report defects, and cooperate with the employer's COSHH arrangements. But the employee also has critical rights: the right to be told what substances they are exposed to, the right to see monitoring results and health surveillance findings (in anonymised form), and the right to refuse work that poses a serious and imminent danger to their health. When an employee is DIAGNOSED with an occupational disease — dermatitis, occupational asthma, hearing loss, or cancer — they enter a complex intersection of employment law, health and safety law, social security law, and civil litigation. Eileen must be able to guide both the employer AND the employee through this.
sections.text:
EMPLOYEE OBLIGATIONS (Reg.8(2)):
(a) Use control measures, PPE, and other provisions correctly.
(b) Return equipment after use and store it properly.
(c) Report any defect in control measures or PPE immediately.
(d) Cooperate with the employer's COSHH arrangements (attend training, follow safe systems of work, submit to health surveillance).
(e) An employee who deliberately ignores control measures may face disciplinary action AND may compromise their own civil claim if they contributed to their exposure.

EMPLOYEE RIGHTS (Reg.12 + HSWA 1974 s.7-8):
(a) To be INFORMED about the hazardous substances they work with and the risks to their health.
(b) To receive TRAINING on how to use control measures and PPE.
(c) To see the results of MONITORING and HEALTH SURVEILLANCE (in anonymised or personal form).
(d) To REFUSE work that poses serious and imminent danger — ERA 1996 s.44 protects the employee from detriment, and s.100 protects from automatic unfair dismissal.
(e) To raise health and safety concerns without fear of detriment — ERA 1996 s.44(1)(c).

WHEN AN EMPLOYEE IS DIAGNOSED WITH AN OCCUPATIONAL DISEASE — THE JOURNEY:

STEP 1 — IMMEDIATE ACTIONS:
(a) Seek medical attention — GP referral to specialist occupational health physician.
(b) Notify the employer IN WRITING — state the diagnosis and the suspected workplace cause.
(c) Request a copy of the COSHH assessment for the substances involved.
(d) Request copies of any monitoring or health surveillance records relating to the employee's exposure.
(e) Keep a personal record of: the diagnosis letter, the substances worked with, the dates of exposure, and the names of any witnesses.

STEP 2 — EMPLOYER'S RESPONSE:
(a) IMMEDIATELY remove the employee from further exposure (or reduce to safe level).
(b) Review and update the COSHH assessment — the diagnosis is EVIDENCE that controls were inadequate.
(c) Refer the employee to occupational health for ongoing assessment.
(d) Check whether the disease is RIDDOR-reportable (Reg.8-9, Schedule 3) — if so, report to HSE.
(e) Review whether OTHER employees are at risk from the same exposure.
(f) Do NOT dismiss or discipline the employee for raising the concern — this would likely constitute automatic unfair dismissal (ERA 1996 s.100) and/or detriment for raising a health and safety concern (ERA 1996 s.44).

STEP 3 — ROUTES TO COMPENSATION:
(a) CIVIL CLAIM for personal injury — the employee may bring a claim against the employer for negligence and/or breach of statutory duty (COSHH). The limitation period is 3 years from the date of knowledge of the disease (Limitation Act 1980 s.11/s.14). For long-latency diseases (cancer, asbestosis), the date of knowledge may be decades after the exposure.
(b) INDUSTRIAL INJURIES DISABLEMENT BENEFIT (IIDB) — a DWP benefit available for prescribed occupational diseases. The claim is made to the DWP, not the employer. It does not affect the civil claim.
(c) PIP (Personal Independence Payment) — if the disease causes significant functional impairment.
(d) STATUTORY SICK PAY — the employee is entitled to SSP for up to 28 weeks.

STEP 4 — LONG-LATENCY DISEASES:
For diseases that manifest decades after exposure (mesothelioma, occupational cancer, chronic silicosis), special considerations apply:
(a) The original employer may no longer exist. The employee should trace the employer's liability insurance using the Employers' Liability Tracing Office (ELTO).
(b) If the insurance cannot be traced, the Diffuse Mesothelioma Payment Scheme 2014 provides lump sum compensation for mesothelioma sufferers who cannot trace a liable employer or insurer.
(c) The Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS) may cover claims where the insurer has become insolvent.
(d) Health surveillance records kept for 40 years (COSHH Reg.11) are critical evidence — they may be the only record of the employee's historical exposure levels.

EILEEN'S ROLE:
Eileen can explain the employee's rights, outline the routes to compensation, and signpost to specialist services. Eileen CANNOT provide legal advice on whether a specific claim would succeed — but she CAN ensure the employee knows: (a) they have a right to claim, (b) the time limits that apply, (c) the evidence they should preserve, and (d) where to find specialist legal help (solicitors specialising in occupational disease claims, many working on a no-win-no-fee basis).
sections.keyPrinciple: COSHH creates obligations on BOTH sides of the employment relationship. The employer must protect; the employee must cooperate and use the protections provided. But when the system fails and an employee develops an occupational disease, Eileen's role shifts from compliance guidance to crisis support — helping the employee understand their rights, preserve evidence, and access the routes to compensation. The 40-year record retention requirement exists PRECISELY because occupational diseases can take decades to manifest.
refs: COSHH 2002 Reg.8(2) (employee obligations)
COSHH 2002 Reg.12 (employee right to information and training)
ERA 1996 s.44 (detriment for raising H&S concerns)
ERA 1996 s.100 (automatic unfair dismissal for H&S reasons)
Limitation Act 1980 s.11/s.14 (3-year limitation — date of knowledge)
Industrial Injuries Disablement Benefit (DWP)
Employers' Liability Tracing Office (ELTO)
Diffuse Mesothelioma Payment Scheme 2014
RIDDOR 2013 Regs.8-9 + Schedule 3 (reportable diseases)
commonErrors: An employee not notifying the employer in writing when diagnosed with a suspected occupational disease — written notification creates a formal record and triggers the employer's obligation to investigate and review the COSHH assessment
An employer dismissing or disciplining an employee who raises a COSHH concern — this is likely automatic unfair dismissal (ERA s.100) and/or detriment (ERA s.44). The employee is protected by statute

An employee assuming they cannot claim because the exposure was years ago — the limitation period runs from the date of KNOWLEDGE of the disease, not the date of exposure. For long-latency diseases, the clock starts when the employee is diagnosed and knows or should know the cause
An employer not checking whether other employees are at risk from the same exposure — a diagnosis in one employee is evidence that the controls are inadequate for ALL employees with similar exposure

An employee not preserving evidence — the employee should keep copies of: the diagnosis letter, safety data sheets, COSHH assessments, monitoring results, payslips showing dates of employment, and the names of colleagues who can confirm the working conditions

Not knowing about ELTO or the Diffuse Mesothelioma Payment Scheme — employees with mesothelioma or other long-latency diseases caused by employers who no longer exist still have routes to compensation

pendingAmendments.status: WELs were updated in 2020 to incorporate binding EU occupational exposure limit values. Post-Brexit, the UK sets its own WELs. Monitor EH40 for further updates.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — WEL changes directly affect employer compliance obligations

pendingAmendments.instrument: HSE enforcement trends — occupational disease prosecutions
pendingAmendments.status: HSE has increased focus on occupational disease prevention, particularly occupational lung disease (silicosis, occupational asthma) and skin disease (dermatitis). Monitor prosecution data.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — occupational disease prosecutions carry significant fines and civil liability
